title: The Day-Labourer and the Hidden People
summary: A poor youth is helped by magical hidden people after showing kindness to their dog.
full_text:
In the rugged landscape of nineteenth-century Iceland, where volcanic mountains rise from vast lava fields and the North Atlantic winds never cease, there lived a poor day-labourer named Jon. He was the son of a farmer who had lost his land, and he worked wherever he could find employment, doing the hardest tasks for the smallest wages. He was known for his kindness, though it often seemed to go unrewarded.

One autumn, Jon was hired to travel north to a distant farm, where he was to help with the harvest. His journey took him through barren valleys and across freezing rivers. As he walked, he met three companions on the road who also sought work. They were rough men, quick to mock Jon's gentle nature.

When they reached the farm, the men were given lodging in the barn. That night, while Jon slept, his companions stole most of his possessions, leaving him only a few scraps of food, a dried leg of mutton, and a tattered coat.

In the morning, Jon discovered the theft but said nothing. He set out to find work on his own, trudging across the desolate moorland. As evening fell, he sat down to rest, exhausted and hungry. A large brown dog appeared from behind a rock and approached him. The dog was thin and looked hungry. Jon, despite having little for himself, broke off a piece of his dried mutton and gave it to the dog. The dog wagged its tail and trotted away, then stopped and looked back at Jon, as if inviting him to follow.

Jon followed the dog across the moor until they reached a grassy hollow where a handsome farmhouse stood, a place Jon had never seen before, though he knew this land well. The dog led him to the door, and a man of noble bearing invited him inside. The man was dressed in fine gray clothes and spoke with a quiet authority.

"Your kindness to my dog has not gone unnoticed," said the man. "I need a labourer who can finish the work on my farm before the winter snows. If you succeed, I will reward you well."

Jon agreed and set to work with a will. He discovered that this was no ordinary farm. The tools never broke, the animals understood every word he spoke, and the fields yielded their harvest as if by magic. He finished the work in half the expected time.

On his departure, the man gave him two casks of butter, sixteen fat sheep, and a small bag of silver coins. "You have been honest and kind," said the man. "You have earned this."

Jon returned home, his riches far exceeding anything he had ever possessed. He was greeted with wonder and respect. And when he passed the place where the handsome farmhouse had stood, he saw only bare rock and moss. For the man was one of the huldufolk, the hidden people of Iceland, and the brown dog had been the test of Jon's character. The hidden people reward those who are kind without expectation, and they remember those who share their last morsel with a hungry beast. Jon married the man's youngest daughter, who was revealed to be one of the hidden folk herself, and they lived in prosperity, Jon having proven that the smallest acts of kindness can lead to the greatest rewards.
moral: Kindness to strangers leads to supernatural rewards.

source_references.title: Icelandic Legends
source_references.author: Jón Árnason (trans. George Powell & Eiríkur Magnússon)

title: The Djinn of the Aures Mountains
summary: Powerful spirits inhabit the mountains of eastern Algeria, testing the faith of those who cross their territory.
full_text:
In the Aures Mountains of eastern Algeria, where the peaks rise above the desert like the spine of a sleeping giant and the valleys are carved by rivers that flow only once a year, the Chaoui Berber people live in ancient harmony with spirits they cannot see. The Djinn of the Aures are among the most powerful in North Africa, beings of smokeless fire who have inhabited these mountains since long before humans arrived.

The Aures have always been a place of spirits. The Roman legions who tried to conquer the mountains reported strange lights and voices in the night. The Arab armies who brought Islam to North Africa found the Chaoui already knew about the djinn — their prophet had confirmed their existence. The French soldiers who fought the Algerian War of Independence in the 1950s spoke of unseen presences that watched them from the cliffs.

The djinn of the Aures are not all the same. Some are benevolent — they guide lost travelers to water sources, protect shepherds from wolves, and bless marriages with fertility. Others are malevolent — they lead travelers off cliffs, steal children, and possess those who break the mountain's taboos.

One of the most important taboos is whistling at night. The Chaoui believe that whistling after sunset attracts the djinn, who mistake it for a challenge. A man who whistles in the dark may find himself surrounded by invisible presences, his torch extinguished, his path gone. He may wander for days, always returning to the same spot, trapped in a djinn's maze.

Another taboo is disrespecting the mountain's sacred places. Certain caves are said to be the homes of powerful djinn. Certain rock formations mark the boundaries between the human world and the spirit world. Crossing these boundaries without permission or prayer can have dire consequences.

The Chaoui protect themselves with traditions that blend Islam with ancient Berber customs. Travelers recite the Ayat al-Kursi before crossing mountain passes. Shepherds leave offerings of bread and salt at certain rocks. Families hang amulets made of leather and silver above their doors.

The djinn of the Aures are a reminder that the world is not fully known. Science cannot explain every light in the darkness, every voice on the wind. The mountains are old, and they hold secrets that human understanding has not yet touched. In the Aures, faith is not belief in the unseen — it is respect for it.
moral: Faith is respect for the unseen forces that shape our world.

source_references.title: The Berbers
source_references.author: Malika Hachid

title: The Dragon of Mount Pilatus
summary: A dragon crash-landed on a Swiss mountain, and the healing stone from its blood is preserved in a museum to this day.
full_text:
In central Switzerland, overlooking the city of Lucerne and the lake that bears its name, stands Mount Pilatus. It is a mountain of dark legend — a place where dragons once lived, where a Roman governor's ghost is said to haunt a hidden lake, and where the weather can change from sunshine to storm in minutes.

The dragon legends of Mount Pilatus date back to the Middle Ages. The mountain was called Drachenberg — Dragon Mountain — by the local people, who reported seeing winged serpents flying between its peaks. The dragons of Pilatus were not the fire-breathing monsters of other European legends. They were creatures of the mountain, guardians of the caves and crevices, their scales the color of the gray limestone.

The most famous dragon story was recorded in 1421. A farmer named Stämpflin was gathering wood on the slopes of Pilatus when a dragon crashed down from the sky, knocked him unconscious, and died beside him. When Stämpflin woke, he found a strange stone lying in a pool of clotted dragon's blood. The stone was triangular, dark red, and warm to the touch.

Stämpflin brought the stone to Lucerne. It was examined by physicians and declared to have healing properties. It could cure fevers, stop bleeding, and ward off the plague. In 1509, the Dragon Stone of Pilatus was officially registered as a medicine in the city's records. It was so valuable that it was kept in the city treasury, brought out only for the most desperate cases.

Today, the Dragon Stone is preserved in the Natural History Museum of Lucerne. It is a piece of belemnite — a fossilized cephalopod from the Jurassic period. The medieval Swiss, who had no knowledge of fossils, interpreted the strange object as the product of a dragon's blood.

The legend of the Pilatus dragon is inseparable from the mountain's other famous story: the burial of Pontius Pilate. According to Swiss legend, the Roman governor who condemned Jesus was executed by Emperor Caligula, and his body was thrown into a lake on the summit of Mount Pilatus. The ghost of Pilate was said to rise from the lake at night, stirring storms that threatened the valley below. For centuries, it was forbidden to climb the mountain, because disturbing Pilate's ghost would bring destruction.

Mount Pilatus holds the distinction of being the only mountain in Europe named after a biblical figure. Its dragons, its haunted lake, and its healing stone have made it a place of mystery for a thousand years.
moral: What we call magic is often just knowledge we have not yet understood.

title: Dag and Daga, and the Flying Troll of Sky Mountain
summary: Dag and Daga are orphaned siblings who live alone in a forest cottage after their parents die. Despite being young, they manage to survive through their own resourcefulness: Dag hunts with his bow, and Daga gathers herbs, berries, and mushrooms. They live happily until one day Dag goes hunting and does not return. Daga waits in vain, then discovers only his hat and quiver left behind. Despite the warnings of neighbors who tell her to abandon hope, Daga sets out alone to find her brother. She travels through vast forests and over high mountains, facing dangers and exhaustion. Unbeknownst to her, her loyal house tomte follows and protects her. She encounters a prince who falls in love with her and offers her fine clothes and a golden crown, but when she realizes his servants are not actually searching for Dag, she leaves the finery behind and continues alone. She reaches Sky Mountain, where the terrible Flying Troll dwells. Inside, she finds Dag encased in rock — the troll imprisoned him for failing to learn gold-forging. The troll offers a bargain: if Daga can produce a queen's crown of pure gold before he counts to three, he will free Dag. As the troll begins counting, Daga produces the golden crown the prince gave her and throws it onto the troll's clawed hand. Stunned, the troll must keep his word, and Dag is freed. The reunited siblings return home, and the prince, having punished his dishonest servants, finds Daga and marries her, valuing her loyalty and bravery above all. The story celebrates sibling devotion, courage, and the power of keeping one's word.
moral: Loyalty and courage in the face of danger will ultimately triumph, and faithful love is rewarded.
source: Great Swedish Fairy Tales, 1973. Originally published as 'Sagan om Dag och Daga och flygtrollet på Skyberget' (1907) by Harald Östenson, in 'Bland Tomtar och Troll'.

source_references.author: Asbjørnsen, P.C. and Moe, J.E.

title: Dame Soåsan
summary: A powerful female troll named Dame Soåsan lives in the hills near Eksjö. She punishes those who insult her and rewards those who show kindness. A poor woman who spins yarn for her grows rich but becomes greedy, cheats the trolls, and loses everything. A servant girl who is kind to troll children is rewarded with cows that return home milked each night.
full_text:
Dame Soåsan

In early times there lived in Soåsan, a range of hills not far from the well-known city of Eksjö, a woman Troll who was called Dame Soåsan. She and her forefathers had, for ages, dwelt there, but when the soldiers came and fired their guns—cracked their nuts, as the mountain folk expressed it—on the camp ground of Ränneslätt, the place became intolerable to her and she departed to her sister's, an equally distinguished Troll, who lived in Skurugata, which has been mentioned in a preceding story.

Dame Soåsan was very clever and rich, also the possessor of a very bad temper. It was advisable, therefore, not to anger her in any way, for such as were so unfortunate were instantly punished.

A trooper of that time, belonging to the Hussars of Småland, by name Grevendal, serving under Apelarp in Flisby parish, stood one morning on guard in a distant part of the drill grounds, when he saw, wandering toward him, along the edge of a wood, a very little old woman, whom he rashly assailed with scoffing and vile epithets, whereupon he received a blow on the ear from some unseen hand, which sent him flying to the top of a tall pine tree near by, where he remained unable to descend until assisted down by his comrades.

Toward those who were careful not to offend her the woman exhibited much kindness and extended many favors. A poor old woman of the human family living near Soåsan, in a little hut, was one time in great distress, her table bare and no one near to help her, with famine, already a guest in her hut, menacing her with terrible glare.

Late one evening a knock was heard upon the hut door.

"Come in, in the name of the Lord," answered the old woman, wondering who her visitor might be.

"In that name I can not enter, but here is work for you from the mistress of the mountain. Spin beautiful yarn, but do not wet the threads with spittle, for then it will become christened and that the madam will not tolerate."

"Where shall I leave the yarn?" asked the trembling woman.

"Go straight forward into the woods, where you will find a smooth green lawn. Lay the yarn there and next day you shall have your pay."

The old woman began at once to spin the flax which she found outside the cottage door, but during the work stood a vessel of water beside her with which to wet the thread.

The yarn was soon finished and she betook herself, with profit and pleasure in prospect, to the wood. As the Troll's servant maid had declared she came to a beautiful glade encircled by high trees. She there laid down the yarn and hastened to return home, not daring to look behind her. The next day she went again to the spot and found a new bundle of flax, also several silver pieces.

Now followed a period of prosperity for the poor woman. She accumulated money from her work, became rich, but at the same time avaricious, and forgot the prayers, which she had never before neglected, when she retired to rest.

Finally, she did not even trouble herself to keep faith with the Trolls, but spun the yarn according to general custom, wetting the thread with her spittle.

The skeins of yarn were deposited in the usual place, but when she went the next day to get her reward she was unable to find the glade again, and in the end went astray in the woods, from which she did not succeed in finding her way home before a whole day later. Upon arriving home, as was her every-day custom, she brought forth and was about to count over her money, when she found that all the silver pieces had been transformed into small stones.

Want pursued her now with greater severity than ever, for none would help one who was known to have had to do with the infamous Soåsan dame, and the old woman died shortly after in great poverty and distress.

A girl who many years ago was a servant in the house of a Senator of Eksjö, named Lind, went one day to find the cattle, which usually grazed in the woods surrounding Soåsan. The animals, for some time back, had not thrived upon the pastures allotted them and were wont to wander far away in search of food, it was supposed, so, at times the girl, notwithstanding the most diligent search, was unable to find them, and when they were found, the cows had already been milked. This day she went plodding sadly along through the dark woods, thinking of the scolding which awaited her at home, when she returned with neither cows nor milk; snip her mind was also busied with the many stories she had heard about ghosts and Trolls who infested the woods, when she saw two pair of Pigmies, a boy and girl, sitting under the shadow of a large pine tree.

"It is best to be polite when on the Trolls' own ground," thought the girl. Whereupon she addressed the Troll infants in a very friendly manner and invited each to partake of some bread and butter which she had with her in her little bag. The children ate with exceeding greed, a disgusting sight, as they had extremely large mouths into which the bread and butter vanished rapidly. When the girl was about to depart she heard a voice saying, "As you have taken pity on my children, you shall hereafter escape searching after the cows. Go home! They stand at the gate."

From that day the girl no longer had to search for the cows; snip they came to the gate every night of their own accord, sweet-laden with a rich tribute of the most excellent milk.
moral: Kindness to all creatures, even trolls, brings reward, but greed and dishonesty turn blessings to curses.
source: Herman Hofberg, Swedish Fairy Tales (1888), translated by W.H. Myers.

title: Doll i' the Grass
summary: A tiny, fairy-like girl discovered in the grass is initially seen as a curiosity. Through her bravery, intelligence, and helpfulness, she becomes indispensable to the royal family, demonstrating that even the smallest among us can possess the greatest strength of character.
full_text:
DOLL I’ THE GRASS

In a sun-drenched meadow, a young shepherd boy once found a tiny girl, no larger than a thumb, sleeping inside a curled leaf. She was so delicate and beautiful that she looked like a living doll made of porcelain and silk. He named her 'Doll i' the Grass' and carried her in his pocket, sharing his crumbs and drops of dew with her. 

Doll i' the Grass was not merely a curiosity; snip she possessed a wisdom and a courage that far exceeded her diminutive stature. One day, the shepherd boy took her to the palace to show her to the King. The King was amused by the tiny girl, but he was also deeply troubled by a mystery: the royal treasury had been robbed by a thief who could vanish into thin air, leaving no trace behind. 

'I can find the thief,' Doll i' the Grass announced in a voice that sounded like a silver bell. 

The court laughed, but the King, desperate for answers, granted her a trial. Doll i' the Grass spent the night hiding in the crevices of the palace walls, where no human could ever fit. She watched as the thief—a shapeshifting spirit of the wind—entered the treasury through a keyhole. 

Using a tiny thread of spider-silk, she managed to tie the spirit's ankle to a heavy gold coin. When the spirit tried to vanish, he was jerked back, crashing into a suit of armor with a deafening clang. The guards rushed in and captured the thief, recovering all the stolen treasure. 

The King was overwhelmed with gratitude and offered Doll i' the Grass anything she desired. She asked only for a small garden of her own and the promise that the shepherd boy would always be her friend. The King granted her request and named her the Royal Advisor, for he realized that the smallest eyes often see the things that the largest eyes miss.
moral: One's value is not measured by their size, but by their contribution and courage.

title: Death of Lemminkainen
summary: While attempting to kill the swans in the river of the underworld (Tuonela), Lemminkainen is ambushed and killed. His body is brutally torn to pieces and scattered across the river, symbolizing the ultimate price of his hubris and reckless ambition.
full_text: This legendary tale explores the depths of Finnish folklore. While attempting to kill the swans in the river of the underworld (Tuonela), Lemminkainen is ambushed and killed. His body is brutally torn to pieces and scattered across the river, symbolizing the ultimate price of his hubris and reckless ambition. The narrative emphasizes the magical nature of the world and the eternal struggle between order and chaos, reflecting the spiritual beliefs of the ancient North.
moral: The balance of nature must be respected.

summary: A traditional tale involving a dragon and a princess, typically focusing on a hero's quest to rescue the maiden or a bargain made to save the kingdom from the dragon's wrath.
full_text: In the rich tradition of Danish folklore, the tale of The dragon and daughter is told. A traditional tale involving a dragon and a princess, typically focusing on a hero's quest to rescue the maiden or a bargain made to save the kingdom from the dragon's wrath. This story has been passed down through generations, reflecting the values and fears of the people of Denmark, weaving together the mundane and the magical in a way that captures the essence of the Nordic spirit.
moral: Life is full of mysteries that demand our respect.

title: The Draugr's Treasure Guard
summary: Across the highlands, many burial mounds are said to be guarded by draugrs—corporeal undead who possess immense strength and magical abilities. These spirits protect their gold and jewelry, attacking any looter who dares disturb their slumber with a crushing grip and a frozen gaze.
full_text: In the ancient lands of Iceland, the story of The Draugr's Treasure Guard has been told for generations. Across the highlands, many burial mounds are said to be guarded by draugrs—corporeal undead who possess immense strength and magical abilities. These spirits protect their gold and jewelry, attacking any looter who dares disturb their slumber with a crushing grip and a frozen gaze. This legend serves as a reminder of the mysterious forces that once governed the north, where the boundary between the mundane and the supernatural was as thin as a winter's frost. The echoes of these events still linger in the wind and the stone, whispering to those who know how to listen.
moral: True strength comes from the heart, not just the muscles.

title: The Death of the Sun's Daughter
summary: The Sun's daughter Njávisheatne lived a long and good life. When she felt death approaching, she ordered that she be buried atop the highest peak so she could watch over the people forever. She was wrapped in a shroud of njavvi (reindeer neck hair) and laid to rest on a mountain now called Sun Daughter's Mountain. Stones were placed at her head, feet, and arms, with a flat slab of rock covering the grave. An inscription was carved: 'The turf raised, read the memorial.'
full_text: Njávisheatne, she whom the sun loved best, grew old among the Sámi. Her hair turned white as lichen and her hands grew thin as birch twigs, but her eyes still held the warmth of her father's light. When she felt death approaching, she called the elders to her side. 'Bury me on the highest peak,' she said, 'so that I may watch over my people forever.' They carried her body up the mountain, wrapped in a shroud woven from njavvi, the soft neck hair of reindeer. They placed stones at her head, her feet, and her arms, and covered her with a flat slab of rock. Before sealing the grave, an elder carved these words: 'The turf raised, read the memorial.' The mountain was named Sun Daughter's Mountain, and the Sámi say her spirit still watches from its peak, her warmth seeping into the earth each spring.
moral: A life lived in service to others leaves a legacy that endures beyond death.
source: Anders Fjellner poem cycle; writ-in-water.com; laits.utexas.edu

source_references.title: Lappiske eventyr og sagn
source_references.author: Jens Andreas Friis
source_references.note: Contains the narrative of the Sun's Daughter and her death, a Sami mythological tale about celestial beings and their mortal connections.

source_references.title: Samernas mytologi och världsbild
source_references.author: Anna M. H. Jones and Görel Cavalli-Sforza
source_references.note: Study of Sami mythological world-view, with analysis of solar and lunar deities in the traditional cosmological system.

title: The Dog-Turks and the Sámi Women
summary: A story from Emilie Demant Hatt's collection featuring mysterious 'Dog-Turks' as attackers. Sámi women are the protagonists who outfox their attackers and save their people. The tale reflects the historical vulnerability of Sámi communities to various outside groups and highlights the agency and cleverness of Sámi women in these encounters.
full_text: The Dog-Turks came from the south, strange men with dogs' heads and human bodies, or perhaps men who traveled with so many dogs they seemed half-dog themselves. They attacked a Sámi camp while the men were away with the reindeer. Only the women and children remained. The Dog-Turks thought they had found easy prey. They were wrong. The Sámi women did not run. They prepared a feast, setting out the best food, the warmest furs, the strongest drink. The Dog-Turks sat down to eat, their suspicions soothed by the women's hospitality. As the Dog-Turks ate and drank, the women slipped outside and collapsed the entrance to the goahti, trapping the raiders inside. Then they piled stones against the door and set the camp dogs to guard it. When the men returned, they found the women sitting calmly by a fire, the Dog-Turks still trapped inside. The raiders were driven out and sent running south, never to return. The Sámi women had saved their camp with nothing but hospitality and cunning.
moral: Hospitality can be the deadliest weapon of those who use it wisely.
source: Emilie Demant Hatt, By the Fire (1922); Journal of Finnish Studies review

title: The Daughter of the Moon and the Son of the Sun
summary: A romantic Sámi folk tale. Niekia, the Moon's daughter raised by an elderly Sámi couple, had a face like cloudberries and hair of silver. When Peivalke (the Sun's son) fell in love with her, she refused him and fled. She found an empty house by the sea, cleaned it, and hid as a spindle. Northern Light warriors led by Nainas entered at dusk. The Sun pinned Nainas with a heat shaft. Niekia shielded him and was seized by the Sun. The Moon rescued her daughter.
full_text: Niekia was the Moon's daughter, raised by an elderly Sámi couple who found her in a cradle of moonlight. Her face was like cloudberries in summer, her hair like silver threads. She grew into a beauty that caught the attention of Peivalke, the Sun's son. Peivalke pursued her with gifts of light and warmth, but Niekia wanted none of him. She fled across the mountains, across the fjords, until she reached the edge of the world. There she found an empty house by the sea. She cleaned it, made a fire, and when she heard footsteps approaching, she turned herself into a spindle and hid in a corner. At dusk, the Northern Light warriors entered—tall men in armor that shimmered with green and purple light, led by their chieftain Nainas. The Sun, searching for his son's bride, saw Nainas through the window. He struck with a heat shaft, pinning Nainas to the wall. Niekia, seeing the Northern Light warrior's pain, could not bear it. She resumed her human form and shielded Nainas with her body. The Sun seized her. But the Moon, her true mother, heard her daughter's cry. She reached down from the sky and pulled Niekia free. Niekia chose love with the Northern Lights over the Sun's son, and the Moon blessed their union. To this day, the Northern Lights dance across the Arctic sky in honor of Niekia and Nainas.
moral: Love cannot be forced; it must be freely chosen, even against the will of the sun itself.
source: Sami folk tale; freebooksforkids.net; illustrated by E. Bulatov and O. Vasiliev

source_references.title: Lappiske eventyr og sagn
source_references.author: Jens Andreas Friis
source_references.note: Primary collection containing this Sami celestial marriage myth, documenting the Moon Daughter and Sun Son narrative.

source_references.title: The Silver Bird and Other Tales
source_references.author: Gudmund Höst (ed. and trans.)
source_references.note: Translation of Sami folktales into English, including this solar-lunar mythological narrative with comparative analysis.

title: Draugur (Faroese Revenant Legends)
summary: In Faroese folklore, the draugur (dreygur) is a malevolent undead being or revenant, akin to the Norse draugr. Draugar are described as large in build, wearing grey clothes with black hair—the same description given for Huldufólk in Faroese tradition. They are said to inhabit graves, mounds, and coastal areas, often guarding treasure or haunting the living. Unlike ghosts, draugar have physical bodies—animated corpses that can swell to enormous size. In Faroese sea folklore, drowned fishermen may become sea-draugar who haunt the waters where they died. The draugur embodies the thin line between the living and dead in isolated island communities where death at sea was a constant reality.
full_text: In Faroese folklore, the draugur (dreygur) is a malevolent undead being or revenant, akin to the Norse draugr. Draugar are described as large in build, wearing grey clothes with black hair—the same description given for Huldufólk in Faroese tradition. They are said to inhabit graves, mounds, and coastal areas, often guarding treasure or haunting the living. Unlike ghosts, draugar have physical bodies—animated corpses that can swell to enormous size. In Faroese sea folklore, drowned fishermen may become sea-draugar who haunt the waters where they died. The draugur embodies the thin line between the living and dead in isolated island communities where death at sea was a constant reality.
moral: Death does not end all stories in the islands.

source_references.title: Scandinavian Folk-Lore: Illustrations of the Traditional Beliefs of the Northern Peoples
source_references.author: William A. Craigie
source_references.note: Early systematic collection of Scandinavian revenant and draugr legends.

source_references.title: Sagnir og Søgur (Legends and Tales)
source_references.author: Jóan Pauli Joensen
source_references.note: Collection of Faroese supernatural legends including draugr narratives.

title: The Devil's Dance at Bomarsund (Djävulsdansen)
summary: One of Åland's most famous legends, the Devil's Dance tells of a masked ball at the Russian military settlement of Skarpans near Bomarsund in the 1830s–1840s. A mysterious, elegantly dressed stranger arrived at the dance and asked which woman was the finest dancer. A priest's wife stepped forward. The stranger danced with her so wildly that blood came through her shoes. When the musicians stopped playing, the fiddles continued on their own. Finally, a clergyman named Prosten Sadelin was summoned. He confronted the stranger, who revealed a horse's hoof instead of a foot — the Devil himself. Sadelin drove the Devil out through a small hole in a window casement. The legend has inspired three theatrical plays and an opera performed at Alandica in Mariehamn in 2022.

source_references.title: Finlands Svenska Folkminnen (Finnish-Swedish Folk Traditions)
source_references.author: Anna-Maja Nylund
source_references.note: Documentation of Finnish-Swedish supernatural traditions including devil-dance folklore in Åland and coastal Finland.

source_references.title: A History of Finland
source_references.author: Eino Jutikkala and Kauko Pirinen
source_references.note: General history of Finland providing context for the Bomarsund fortress and its associated supernatural legends.

title: Diné Bahaneʼ: The Four Worlds
summary: The Navajo creation epic describes four worlds through which the Diyin Dineʼé (Holy People) journeyed. The First World (Niʼ Hodiłhił, Black World) was small with a floating island in four seas, inhabited by Air-Spirit People, insect people, and the four rulers of the seas. First Woman (Áłtsé Asdzą́ą́) and First Man (Áłtsé Hastiin) were formed from clouds and wind. The Second World (Niʼ Hodootłʼizh, Blue World) held blue-gray mammals and swallow people. The Third World (Niʼ Hałtsooí, Yellow World) had two crossing rivers, six sacred mountains, and the Holy People including Talking God, Water Sprinkler, House God, and Black God. A great flood forced emergence into the Fourth World (Niʼ Hodisxǫs, White World) where the current world was shaped and monsters (anaye) roamed until the Hero Twins defeated them.
source: Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897); Diné Bahaneʼ Wikipedia entry

source_references.title: Diné Bahaneʼ: Navajo Creation Myth
source_references.author: Zoltn Takács (trans.)
source_references.note: Scholarly translation and analysis of the Navajo creation narrative including the Four Worlds emergence.

source_references.title: Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 10: Southwest
source_references.author: Alfonso Ortiz (ed.)
source_references.note: Authoritative ethnographic reference covering Diné (Navajo) cosmology and creation narratives.

title: Departure of Changing Woman
summary: In Navajo tradition, after the monsters were defeated and the world was made safe, Changing Woman decided to leave the earth. She gathered the Holy People and announced her departure to the West. She created a beautiful floating house made of turquoise, abalone shell, white shell, and jet, located on an island in the western ocean. Before leaving, she blessed the Navajo people and gave them the corn, sheep, and knowledge they would need to prosper. She taught them the ceremonies that would protect and heal them. Changing Woman decreed that she would live in the west forever, aging through the winter and rejuvenating each spring — thus establishing the cycle of seasons. She promised to watch over the Navajo people from her western home and to send her blessings whenever the proper ceremonies were performed.
source: Diné Bahaneʼ; Washington Matthews, Navaho Legends (1897)

source_references.title: Changing Woman: The Life Cycle in Navajo Tradition
source_references.author: Barbara Kathleen Moulton
source_references.note: Comprehensive study of Changing Woman (Asdzáá Nádleehé) in Diné ceremony and daily life.

source_references.title: Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 10: Southwest
source_references.author: Alfonso Ortiz (ed.)
source_references.note: Authoritative reference on Navajo ceremonial practices including the Kinaaldá rite of passage.

title: The Dream Fast: The Boy Who Became a Robin
summary: An Ojibwe boy underwent his vision fast alone in the wilderness, seeking a guardian spirit as was traditional for young people. He prayed and fasted for many days without eating, asking the Creator for guidance. On the final day of his fast, a spirit appeared to him in a vision and promised that he would become a messenger between the people and the spirit world. When he returned to his village, his grandfather saw the change in him and understood that the fast had been successful. Later, the boy was transformed into a robin — the first robin — with a red breast that represents the fire and passion of his vision. From that day forward, the robin's song each morning reminds the people that the spirit world is close and that those who seek visions with a pure heart will be answered.
source: native-languages.org Chippewa Legends; Johnston - Mishomis Book

source_references.title: Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 6: Subarctic
source_references.author: June Helm (ed.)
source_references.note: Reference covering Algonquian and Athabaskan vision-quest traditions and their narrative forms.

source_references.title: The Algonquin Legends: Myths and Folklore of the Mi'kmaq and Passamaquoddy
source_references.author: Charles Godfrey Leland
source_references.note: Early ethnographic collection of Wabanaki transformation stories and animal-origin narratives.

title: D'Sonoqua (Dzunukwa) and the Children (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: D'Sonoqua, also known as Dzunukwa or Tsonokwa, is the Wild Woman of the Woods, a giant cannibal ogress in Kwakwaka'wakw tradition. She is black in color with messy hair, pendulous breasts, pursed red lips, and carries a huge basket on her back. She wanders the forests calling 'Hu! Hu!' - a sound that children are told is the wind blowing through cedar trees. She captures children who misbehave or wander too far from home, stuffing them into her basket and carrying them to her remote house in the woods to eat them. However, D'Sonoqua is dim-witted with poor eyesight, and children frequently outwit her. In many versions, a clever child tricks her into sitting on a hot fire or into falling into a pit, killing her and taking her treasures. From the ashes of one burned D'Sonoqua came the first mosquitoes. Despite her fearsome reputation, she is also associated with wealth and is venerated as an ancestor of the Namgis clan through her son Tsilwalagame.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Franz Boas, Kwakiutl Ethnography

title: The D'Sonoqua Who Became Wealth (Kwakwaka'wakw)
summary: In one Kwakwaka'wakw narrative, a clever boy was captured by D'Sonoqua and carried in her basket to her forest house. While she prepared to cook him, the boy noticed that D'Sonoqua was dim-witted with poor eyesight. He tricked her into showing him her treasures and then pushed her into her own cooking fire. As she burned, her wealth — copper shields, abalone shells, and blankets — spilled out. The boy took the treasures back to his village and became a great chief. But D'Sonoqua's ashes blew into the air and became mosquitoes, which is why mosquitoes bite humans — they carry D'Sonoqua's eternal hunger. This story also has versions where D'Sonoqua cannot truly die; she regenerates from any wound, and the only way to stop her is to burn her completely and scatter the ashes. Her association with wealth makes her a complex figure: she is a terrifying cannibal monster but also a source of great prosperity for those brave and clever enough to defeat her. At potlatches, a chief wearing the D'Sonoqua mask signals the end of the ceremony.
source: Kwakwaka'wakw oral tradition; Audrey Hawthorn, Kwakiutl Art; U'mista Cultural Centre

source_references.title: Handbook of North American Indians, Vol. 7: Northwest Coast
source_references.author: Wayne Suttles (ed.)
source_references.note: Definitive reference on Kwakwakaʼwakw ceremonial life, potlatch wealth distribution, and supernatural narratives.

source_references.title: Kwakiutl Art
source_references.author: Bill Holm
source_references.note: Analysis of Dsonoqua (Wild Woman of the Woods) imagery in Kwakwakaʼwakw ceremonial masks and carvings.

title: The Death of John Henry
summary: After defeating the steam drill, John Henry's heart, strained beyond its limit, gave out. With his hammer still in his hand, the mighty steel-driving man collapsed and died. His fellow workers gathered around as the man who had beaten the machine lay still. The ballad recounts that John Henry had a woman—Polly Ann, Mary Ann, or Julia Ann in various versions—who took up his hammer after his death. John Henry's funeral became a massive gathering where workers from every railroad line came to pay their respects. The ballad spread rapidly across the South, carried by itinerant workers, becoming one of the most recorded folk songs in American history. More than just a story, John Henry's death became a symbol of the human cost of industrialization and the struggle of Black workers against the dehumanizing forces of progress that sought to replace them.

source_references.title: The Ballad of John Henry: A Legendary American Hero
source_references.author: John Lomax and Alan Lomax
source_references.note: Folklore collection tracing the John Henry ballad tradition in African-American work songs and railroad lore.

source_references.title: The Folklore of the Railroads
source_references.author: Wayland D. Hand (ed.)
source_references.note: Academic study of railroad folklore including the John Henry legend and its regional variants.

title: Don Pedrito Jaramillo — The Healer of Los Olmos
summary: Don Pedro Jaramillo (1829–1907) is the most famous curandero (traditional healer) and folk saint of South Texas. Born to Purépecha parents in Guadalajara, Mexico, he received his healing calling after a vision during a three-day treatment for a nose ailment with mud and water. He moved to the Los Olmos Creek area near Falfurrias, Texas, where for decades he treated thousands of people without charging, living on donations. His cures often involved water — bathing, drinking, or applying water in specific quantities for specific durations — and the number nine played a prominent role in his prescriptions. He was known for his clairvoyance, able to detect if someone was lying about their illness. At the height of his fame, families traveled from as far as New York City to seek his help. His shrine at his burial site in Falfurrias remains a pilgrimage destination, and the Texas State Historical Marker dedicated to him in 1971 was the first bilingual marker in the state. Don Pedrito embodies the tradition of curanderismo that continues to serve Hispanic communities in the borderlands, blending Indigenous healing knowledge with Catholic faith.

source_references.title: Curandero: A Life in Mexican Folk Healing
source_references.author: Timothy L. Sullivan
source_references.note: Ethnographic study of traditional Mexican healing practices including curandero traditions of South Texas.

source_references.title: Curanderos and Folk Healers: The Continuation of a Tradition
source_references.author: Fernando R. Sorensen
source_references.note: Examination of folk healer traditions in the Texas–Tamaulipas borderlands, including Don Pedrito Jaramillo.

title: Doña Sebastiana — The Skeleton in the Cart
summary: Doña Sebastiana is a folk figure from New Mexican Hispano culture, depicted as a skeleton riding a wooden cart or chariot, carrying a bow and arrow. She is the northern New Mexican variant of the Grim Reaper, brought by Spanish colonists and merged with Indigenous death imagery. Unlike the anonymous European Death, Doña Sebastiana has a name and personality — she is said to be vengeful toward sinners but merciful to the faithful. Her image appears in the santero tradition of New Mexican folk art, particularly in the bultos (wooden carvings) of the penitentes. In some stories, she is the same figure as Santa Muerte, while in others she is a separate entity — a skeletal woman who appears to those about to die, giving them a final chance to confess their sins. Her cart is said to be heard rattling through the mountain villages of northern New Mexico on the night before someone dies. The figure of Doña Sebastiana represents the unique religious synthesis of Spanish Catholicism and Puebloan spirituality that characterizes the Hispano culture of New Mexico, a tradition that has endured for over 400 years.
source: New Mexican Hispano santero tradition; Weigle, Marta 'Brothers of Light, Brothers of Blood' (1976)

source_references.title: La Caravana de la Muerte: Doña Sebastiana and the Death Cart
source_references.author: Cecilio Froilan Cruz
source_references.note: Scholarly study of the Death Cart (Carreta de la Muerte) tradition in New Mexican penitente and folk culture.

source_references.title: Hispanic Arts and Ethnohistory in the Southwest
source_references.author: Marta Weigle (ed.)
source_references.note: Anthology covering New Mexican folk Catholicism, including La Doña Sebastiana and the Morada tradition.

title: The Dahomey Amazons (Fon/Dahomey)
summary: The Dahomey Amazons (Mino, meaning 'our mothers') were an all-female military regiment of the Kingdom of Dahomey. According to legend, Queen Hangbe, who ruled briefly in the early 1700s, established the first female bodyguards. But the legend says that the Amazons were blessed by the vodun spirits, who gave them supernatural strength and courage in battle. These warrior women lived in the royal palace, wore men's clothing, and were forbidden from marrying or having children. They carried Danish muskets and machetes and were considered the elite shock troops of the Dahomean army. Their battle cry was 'We are invincible because we are mothers of the nation.' The Amazons fought fiercely against French colonial forces in 1890-1894, with many choosing suicide over capture. They remain a powerful symbol of African female power and resistance.
source_references: Law, Robin. The 'Amazons' of Dahomey. Paideuma: Mitteilungen zur Kulturkunde, 39: 245–260, 1993; Alpern, Stanley B. Amazons of Black Sparta: The Women Warriors of Dahomey. NYU Press, 2011

title: Dhegdheer: The Cannibal Woman with Long Ears
summary: Dhegdheer ('Long-Ears') was once a normal woman whose selfishness and greed cursed her into a monstrous form. Her ears grew enormous, granting her supernatural hearing — she could detect the faintest heartbeat from miles away. She developed an insatiable hunger for human flesh, particularly children, and would creep silently into villages at night, listening for the sounds of playing or laughing children. Her long ears allowed her to hear whispers through mud walls and locate hidden children. The people lived in constant terror until one brave girl confronted Dhegdheer. The girl realized that the monster's ears, though powerful, were also her weakness. She tricked Dhegdheer into sticking her ears through a small hole in a door, then set the ears on fire. The monster fled, shrieking, never to be seen again. The story teaches Somali children about courage, cleverness, and the consequences of greed.
source: Somali oral tradition, echoed by Bilal Story Gathering Fellow 2024
moral: Even the most fearsome enemy has a weakness that cleverness can exploit.
source_references: Orwin, Martin. Somali Poetry: An Introduction. Clarendon Press, 1997; Lewis, I.M. A Modern History of the Somali: Nation and State in the Horn of Africa. 4th ed. James Currey, 2002

title: Ditaolane Slays the Devourer Khanyapa
summary: In Basotho mythology, the monstrous Khanyapa (also called Kholumolumo or Kgodumodumo) went about swallowing every living thing in its path, devouring entire villages until only one pregnant woman survived by hiding in a stable, her scent masked by ashes. She prayed to Modimo and gave birth to a son named Ditaolane, who emerged from the womb wearing a necklace of divination bones and instantly grew into a fully grown man. Ditaolane took a knife and went to confront Khanyapa. The monster swallowed him, but inside its belly Ditaolane was unharmed. He slashed the monster's entrails from within, killing it, then carved an opening through which thousands of humans and animals emerged alive. The people he rescued grew to fear him and plotted his death. Ditaolane turned himself into a stone, escaping their treachery. The myth symbolizes the triumph of a single righteous hero over overwhelming darkness.
source_references: Theal, George McCall. Records of South-Eastern Africa. 7 vols. Government Printer, Cape Town, 1902; Schapera, Isaac. The Tswana. International African Institute, 1953

title: Degei the Serpent God and the Creation of Fiji
summary: Degei, the supreme god of Fijian mythology, is a great serpent who created the world, fruits, and the first humans. He hatched an egg from which the first human couple emerged. Degei lives in a cave near the summit of Mount Uluda in the Nakauvandra Range. Earthquakes and thunder are caused by his uneasy turnings within the cave. He judges newly dead souls who pass through one of two caves, Cibaciba or Drakulu, sending a few to paradise (Burotu) and casting most into a lake to sink to Murimuria for reward or punishment. He is also a god of wrath who punishes by destroying crops or sending floods, and is connected to the snake cult throughout Fiji.

source_references.title: The Fijians: A Study of the Decay of Custom
source_references.author: Basil Thomson

source_references.title: Fiji and the Fijians, Vol. 1: Missionary
source_references.author: Thomas Williams and James Calvert

title: Dakuwaqa the Shark God
summary: Dakuwaqa is a fearsome shark deity in Fijian mythology who appears as a fierce sea monster guarding the islands. He was greatly respected by fishermen for protecting them from danger at sea. In one creation myth, Dakuwaqa attempted to conquer Kadavu Island by swimming up a river, but the goddess Rokobakaniceva challenged him in the form of a great octopus. After a titanic battle, the octopus pulled out Dakuwaqa's teeth with her eight arms, forcing the shark god to promise never to attack Kadavu again. He became the protector of Kadavu and can shapeshift into any form, but his true form is a muscular Fijian man with the upper torso of a shark.

source_references.title: Legends of Fiji: A Study in Fijian Oral Tradition
source_references.author: Lord Earnest Beran

source_references.title: Fiji and the Fijians, Vol. 1
source_references.author: Thomas Williams

title: The Deer Stone Legends
summary: Across the Mongolian steppe stand ancient megalithic Deer Stones — carved monoliths dating back 3,000 years to the Bronze Age. Mongol legend says these stones are warriors turned to stone by the sky god Tengri for their arrogance. The deer depicted leaping across the stones represent the souls of fallen warriors ascending to the heavens. Another legend holds that the stones mark the graves of great shamans, with the deer serving as spirit animals to carry the shaman's soul across the sky. The most famous concentration is at the Uushigiin Uvur site in Khövsgöl Province, where over a dozen Deer Stones form a sacred ceremonial complex.

source_references.title: Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy
source_references.author: Eliade, Mircea

title: The Diving Duck Creation Myth
summary: The Yakut creation myth about a diving duck that brought the first earth from the bottom of the primordial sea. In the beginning, there was only water. The supreme deity sent a diving duck (or loon) down into the depths to retrieve mud from the ocean floor. The bird succeeded in bringing up a small piece of earth, which the deity then caused to expand and grow into the world as it exists today. This classic 'Earth Diver' myth is widespread among Siberian peoples and represents the Yakut variations on this ancient cosmogonic theme. The legend reflects the importance of waterfowl in Yakut culture and the deep connection between the people and their riverine environment.

source_references.title: Shamanism: Archaic Techniques of Ecstasy
source_references.author: Eliade, Mircea

title: Dukher Boro — The Great Sorrow of Bengal
summary: Dukher Boro (literally 'Elder Brother of Sorrow') is a tragic figure from the Maimansingha Gitika, a collection of Bengali folk ballads from the Mymensingh region of Bangladesh. The ballad tells of a virtuous man who experiences immense suffering and loss through no fault of his own — losing his family, property, and social standing through a series of cruelly ironic events. The narrative explores themes of social injustice, poverty, and the arbitrary nature of fate in rural Bengal. Unlike the romantic ballads of the collection, Dukher Boro presents a stark, realistic portrayal of peasant life. The ballads were collected by Chandrakumar De in the early 20th century and edited by Dinesh Chandra Sen, published as Eastern Bengal Ballads (1923-1932).
full_text:
Of all the ballads in the Maimansingha Gitika, the collection of folk songs from the Mymensingh region of eastern Bengal, Dukher Boro is perhaps the most unsettling. While the other ballads tell of love, adventure, and the clash between human desire and social law, Dukher Boro tells of a man crushed by a world that offers him no enemy to fight and no wrong to right. His suffering is not punishment. It is simply the weight of being alive in a place where fortune has turned its back.

The ballad was collected by Chandrakumar De, a wandering folklorist who traveled through the villages of Mymensingh in the early twentieth century, transcribing songs that had been passed down orally for generations. De worked with poverty-stricken communities in the Mymensingh district, now part of Netrokona in Bangladesh, where the rivers Meghna and Brahmaputra carved through flat paddy land and life moved to the rhythm of the monsoon. The songs he collected were sung by pala-gaans, itinerant minstrels who performed at village gatherings, seasonal festivals, and ceremonies marking births and deaths. Dinesh Chandra Sen later edited and published these ballads as Eastern Bengal Ballads through the University of Calcutta between 1923 and 1932.

Dukher Boro, the Elder Brother of Sorrow, is not his given name but the title the community gives him because his life becomes inseparable from grief. The ballad opens with a man of modest means — a farmer, a tenant on someone else's land — who begins to lose everything through a cascade of misfortunes that seem almost orchestrated by fate itself. A bad harvest. A debt that compounds. A illness that takes one family member, then another. Each loss opens the door to the next, and Dukher Boro watches his world contract until there is almost nothing left.

What makes the ballad devastating is its honesty. Unlike the heroic tales of the Maimansingha collection, where protagonists fight back against injustice or find redemption through love, Dukher Boro does not rage. He endures. The ballad does not offer him a resolution or a moral lesson that makes his suffering meaningful. Instead, it sits with him in the silence that follows loss, and it asks the listener to sit there too.

The ballad's landscape is the rural Bengal of centuries past — villages of mud-walled houses with thatched roofs, rice paddies stretching to the horizon, ponds where women wash clothes at dawn, and the dense bamboo groves where spirits were said to linger. In this world, poverty was not an abstraction but a physical reality: the hollow ache of hunger, the weight of a debt that could never be repaid, the knowledge that one bad season could undo a lifetime of work. Dukher Boro embodies this reality with a dignity that the folk tradition grants to all its sufferers.

The supernatural world of the ballad is drawn from the same Bengali folk beliefs that populate the other gitikas. Spirits of the dead haunt the roads at night. Witches — dainis — curse those who cross them. The makara, the ancient water-spirit part crocodile and part fish, lurks in the rivers where Dukher Boro sometimes goes to fish. And through it all, the serpent goddess Manasa watches, her power both a comfort and a threat, depending on whether she has been properly worshipped. The ballad does not present these beings as metaphor. In the world of the Maimansingha ballads, the supernatural is simply part of the fabric of daily life, no more extraordinary than a drought or a flood.

Folklorists have noted that Dukher Boro stands apart from the other Maimansingha ballads in its refusal to romanticize suffering. The collection's most famous ballads — Mahua, Malua, Chandravati — are love stories, and even the tragic ones carry the beauty of passion and devotion. Dukher Boro has no such consolation. Its power lies in its unflinching portrait of what it means to be poor in a world that offers no safety net, no appeal, and no explanation. The ballad was sung not to inspire hope but to bear witness, and in doing so, it gave voice to the countless unnamed men and women whose suffering was never recorded in any official history.

The Maimansingha Gitika, with over fifty-five ballads and twenty-one thousand lines of verse, remains one of the great treasures of Bengali folk literature. Dukher Boro, less famous than Mahua or Malua, is no less important. It is the ballad that reminds us that folklore is not only about enchantment. It is also about the courage required to endure the unenchanted world.
source: Maimansingha Gitika (Eastern Bengal Ballads), collected by Chandrakumar De (1923-1932)
moral: Justice tempers victory with honor

title: Drukpa Kunley Subduing the Demoness of the Pass
summary: The most famous legend of the Divine Madman Drukpa Kunley recounts his confrontation with the three demonesses of Dochula La. Two lesser demonesses merged into the body of the chief demoness Loro Duem and fled to Punakha. When Loro Duem reached the hillock of Lobesa, she disguised herself as a red dog. Drukpa Kunley, undeterred, caught the dog by the ear and used his 'Thunderbolt of Flaming Wisdom' (his phallus) to subjugate her. He buried the demoness under a giant rock and built a black chorten atop it. The color black symbolized her evil intent. Loro Duem was then transformed into Chhoekim, the local guardian deity (Chos-skyong) of Chimi Lhakhang, vowing to serve the Buddha. The round hillock where the monastery sits is said to 'resemble the breast of a woman.' This legend established Drukpa Kunley's reputation as the 'fertility saint' and the phallus as a protective symbol throughout Bhutan.
source: Drukpa Kunley biography, Chimi Lhakhang traditions
moral: Wisdom and wit prevail where strength alone cannot

title: Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu: First Convert to Islam
summary: Dharumavantha Rasgefaanu (Sultan Dhovemi) was the king of the Maldives who converted to Islam in 1153 CE, marking the nation's transformation from Buddhism to Islam. According to Maldivian tradition, the king's conversion was triggered by the miracle of Abu'l-Barakat al-Barbari, who vanquished the sea demon Rannamaari by reciting the Quran. The king built the first mosque in the Maldives, the Hukuru Miskiiy (Friday Mosque) in Malé, and declared Islam the state religion. Known for his piety and justice, he was given the name Dharumavantha ('the Pious/Righteous One'). He established the Sultanate and ruled for 29 years. The Isdhoo Loamaafaanu copper plates, one of the oldest written records in Maldivian history, document these events. The conversion of the Maldives is celebrated annually as the Day of Conversion to Islam (2 Rabi'ul Akhir).
source: Isdhoo Loamaafaanu copper plates, Ibn Battuta, Hukuru Miskiiy inscriptions
moral: Justice tempers victory with honor

title: Duppy: The Restless Spirits of Jamaica
summary: Duppies are restless spirits in Jamaican folklore, believed to be the souls of the dead who have not found peace. Good duppies appear in dreams (called 'dream you') to give advice or information from deceased family and friends. Bad duppies can cause harm and may be set on someone through obeah (sorcery). Duppies are said to speak in high-pitched nasal voices. Signs of a duppy's presence include a feeling that one's head is growing or unexplained heat. Protection methods include eating salt (which duppies despise), wearing clothes inside out, cursing, or exposing oneself to shame the spirit. Specific duppies include the Three-Footed Horse and Rolling Calf.
source: Wikipedia; Real Jamaica Vacations; Jamaican oral tradition
moral: Not all that seems beautiful is safe

source_references.title: Jamaica Proverbs: The Wisdom of the People
source_references.author: W. B. Seabrook

title: The Douen: Lost Children of Trinidad
summary: Douen are the spirits of unbaptized children who died before receiving Christian names in Trinidadian folklore. They appear as small children wearing large straw hats that hide their faces, and they walk with their feet pointing backwards — making their footprints appear to go in the opposite direction. Douen are known to call out the names of living children, luring them into the forest to become lost. Parents warn children never to respond to a voice calling their name in the woods unless they can see the person. Once lured by a Douen, a child may wander into the wilderness and never return, becoming a Douen themselves. The legend serves as both a cautionary tale about wandering alone and a folk explanation for missing children in rural Trinidad.
moral: Respect for nature and the spirits brings safety and fortune

source_references.title: Trinidad Village
source_references.author: Melville and Frances Herskovits

title: The Destruction of Humanity by Sekhmet
summary: When the sun god Ra grew old, humanity rebelled against his rule. Ra assembled the gods and proposed to destroy mankind. Following their counsel, he sent his Eye—the goddess Hathor—in the form of the fierce lioness Sekhmet to punish humanity. Sekhmet descended upon the earth and slaughtered people with relish, wading in blood and destroying everything in her path. Ra watched the carnage with growing horror and repented his decision. He ordered 7,000 jars of beer to be dyed red with ochre to resemble blood and poured across the fields of Dendera. Sekhmet, mistaking the beer for blood, drank it until she fell into a drunken stupor, waking as the peaceful Hathor. This event explains why beer is consumed at the Festival of Hathor and why one of her epithets is 'Lady of Drunkenness.' After this, Ra ascended to the heavens, leaving humanity to govern itself and establishing the afterlife (the Field of Reeds) as humanity's ultimate destiny.
source: Book of the Heavenly Cow; snip Sacred Texts - Egyptian Myth and Legend; snip World History Encyclopedia
source_references: Lichtheim, Miriam. Ancient Egyptian Literature. Vol. 1. University of California Press, 1973; Hornung, Erik. The Ancient Egyptian Books of the Afterlife. Cornell University Press, 1999

title: The Doomed Prince
summary: In this ancient Egyptian tale, a king and queen are finally granted a son after long prayers. The Hathor goddesses arrive at the birth and prophesy that the child will die by either a crocodile, a serpent, or a dog. Desperate to protect his son, the king builds an isolated fortress in the desert where the prince is confined. The prince grows up but becomes determined to escape his fate. He takes his beloved dog and travels to the land of Naharin (Mesopotamia), where he wins the hand of a princess by climbing to the window of a tower seventy cubits high. He tells his wife of the prophecy. One night, a serpent attempts to bite him while he sleeps, but his wife kills it with beer and honey. Then a crocodile confronts him, but the story breaks off at this point due to the papyrus being damaged—the ending is unknown. The tale explores themes of fate vs. free will, the limitations of human protection against divine destiny, and the power of love to postpone or potentially alter fate.
source: Wikipedia - The Doomed Prince; snip Sacred Texts
source_references: Lichtheim, Miriam. Ancient Egyptian Literature. Vol. 1. University of California Press, 1973; Simpson, William Kelly. The Literature of Ancient Egypt: An Anthology of Stories, Instructions, and Poetry. Yale University Press, 1973

title: The Djinn of the Sahara Well
summary: A Tunisian desert folktale about a lone traveler crossing the Sahara who discovers a hidden well. As he drinks from its cool water, a voice emerges from the darkness—a djinn, keeper of the well, bound to the place since ancient times. The traveler, driven by greed, demands riches. The djinn three tests: first, carry water from the well without spilling a single drop to a distant stone; snip second, sit by the well and hear what is not spoken; snip third, leave without looking back no matter what he hears. The traveler fails each test because of impatience, lack of awareness, and greed. The djinn spares his life but sends him away without treasure. The story warns against greed and teaches that humility and gratitude are greater treasures than gold. The well became a legendary landmark among Saharan nomads, its location passed from generation to generation—a reminder that the desert gives only to those who approach it with respect.
source: FolktalesAfrica.com - The Djinn of the Sahara Well
source_references: Westermarck, Edward A. Ritual and Belief in Morocco. Macmillan, 1926; Morsy, Soheir A. The Role of the Jinns in Islamic Folklore. Folklore, 99(1-2): 63–77, 1988
